evidence.fetched_text:
Blakes Hotel in London remains closed with future uncertain 

 

 

The future of Blakes Hotel in London’s South Kensington is uncertain, as the property remains closed for business.

The five-star hotel closed on May 31, 2022, for a renovation and was scheduled to reopen on July 31, 2023. However, its doors remain shut two years later.

The hotel’s website is still active, but there is a note on it which reads ‘temporarily closed’. Emails cannot be delivered to the hotel and its phone number rings through to a standard O2 voicemail message.

 

Blakes was previously a member of Design Hotels, a 300+ strong collection of design-led properties around the world. However, a spokesperson confirmed to Boutique Hotelier that the hotel has not been a part of Design Hotels since 2022.

An FAQ section on Blakes Hotel’s website says the property is ‘proudly owned by a private independent entity’.

Boutique Hotelier understands this to be Blakes Management Limited. Companies House filings show that entrepreneur Meir Abutbul and hotelier Navid Mirtorabi–who rescued Blakes from administration in 2010–both resigned as directors of the firm in 2021.

Adrian Gerard John Olivero and Thuy Anh Nguyen Phan, hotel business director at TNG Holdings Vietnam, are listed as active directors.

Blakes, which is considered one of the world’s first luxury boutique hotels, first opened in 1978 under the vision of celebrated interior designer Anouska Hempel.

The hotel earned a reputation for discrection and welcomed A-list guests including Princess Margaret, actresses Gwyneth Paltrow and Lindsay Lohan; and supermodel Kate Moss.

Blakes was acquired by buy-to-let firm GuestInvest in 2007 but plans to adapt it to the firm’s model never materialised, with GuestInvest being placed into administration the following year.

In 2010, Blakes was rescued from administration in a £20m deal by entrepreneur Meir Abutbul and hotelier Navid Mirtorabi, the latter of which now owns hotel and members’ club brand The Twenty Two, which boasts sites in London and New York.

The pair undertook a major refurbishment of the property and brought back Hempel as a design consulant to work on the project.

Boutique Hotelier has reached out to Blakes Hotel for comment

Blakes Hotel

Blakes Hotel is a 5-star hotel in London, and considered one of the world's first boutique hotels. It is at 33 Roland Gardens in South Kensington. It was established by London hotelier and designer Anouska Hempel, and contains eclectic artifacts collected by Hempel during her international travels. Known for protecting its clients' privacy from the paparazzi, it often accommodates celebrities. Regular guests have included Princess Margaret; Sarah Ferguson; actresses Gwyneth Paltrow and Lindsay Lohan; and supermodel Kate Moss..

The hotel is also home to a Mediterranean-style restaurant and an exclusive lounge named Blakes Below, which debuted in 2016.

The hotel was mentioned as a plot point in Sofia Coppola's 2020 film On the Rocks.

The hotel has been closed since May 31, 2022.

## Interior

The hotel's 45 rooms were designed with varied influences; Asian, Moroccan and Colonial styles are conspicuous.

The single rooms contain French double beds with blacks and whites in an eclectic style. The double rooms are styled as Indenture or Provençal design, including antique swan beds. The Director's Double has European king-sized beds which are either French gilded antique swan or four-poster. The Luxury Suites, decorated in various styles, have European king-sized four-poster beds with extra high ceilings and their own balconies with French windows. The Library Suite has hidden cupboards behind fake bookshelves, silk wall hangings, and a double-height four-poster bed.

# Blakes Hotel

Ara Eve Leo Sal

1x

Blakes Hotel is a pioneering luxury boutique hotel in South Kensington, London, established in 1978 by designer and former owner Anouska Hempel, and widely regarded as the world's first of its kind, blending eclectic, theatrical interiors with impeccable service in a series of converted Victorian townhouses.[1][2] Renowned for redefining hospitality through its unconventional luxury and bohemian elegance, the hotel features 44 uniquely designed rooms, including five suites, each showcasing Hempel's signature style—from opulent four-poster beds and antique Asian-inspired furnishings to airy, mother-of-pearl accented spaces—creating a sensual, maze-like atmosphere scented with grapefruit candles.[2][3] Its ground-floor restaurant evokes an Art Deco ambiance with a menu fusing Asian flavors and comforting dishes, such as black cod, sashimi, and truffle tagliolini, while a secluded courtyard oasis adds a vibrant Moroccan-tiled retreat amid potted plants.[2] Although lacking an on-site gym or spa, Blakes partnered with nearby wellness facilities like the South Kensington Club, and offered curated packages such as the Honey and Lavender experience, which included beekeeper consultations, infused tastings, and massages.[2] The hotel closed for renovations on 31 May 2022 and remains closed as of 2023, with its future uncertain.[4] Tucked away on Roland Gardens in the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, moments from cultural landmarks like the V&A Museum and Royal Albert Hall, the hotel was a coveted haven for creative elites in fashion, film, and art, maintaining its timeless allure over four decades.[1][2]

## History

### Founding and Early Development

Blakes Hotel was established in 1978 by Anouska Hempel, a New Zealand-born former actress and emerging interior designer who had gained recognition for her role as a Bond girl in the 1969 film On Her Majesty's Secret Service.[https://www.vanityfair.com/style/2015/04/anouska-hempel-lady-weinberg-book] Drawing on her creative background, Hempel envisioned a departure from traditional grand hotels, pioneering the boutique hospitality model that emphasized personalized, artistic experiences over standardized luxury.[https://hospitalitydesign.com/people/podcasts/anouska-hempel/] The hotel originated from Hempel's ambitious conversion of a cluster of 19th-century Victorian townhouses located at 33 Roland Gardens in South Kensington, London, transforming them into a compact 45-room luxury property.[https://magazine.tablethotels.com/en/2018/04/golden-age/] This project, completed and opened in 1978, is widely credited as the world's first boutique hotel, introducing an intimate scale and eclectic aesthetic that influenced global hospitality trends.[https://www.telegraph.co.uk/travel/destinations/europe/united-kingdom/england/london/articles/blakes-hotel-turns-40/] Hempel's hands-on involvement was central to the early development, as she personally oversaw the design and curation process, blending architectural reconfiguration with curated furnishings to create a theatrical yet inviting atmosphere.[https://www.courtneyprice.com/blakes-hotel-london/] Despite the logistical hurdles of adapting historic structures for modern hotel use, her vision established Blakes as a landmark in innovative lodging from its inception.[https://magazine.tablethotels.com/en/2018/04/golden-age/]

### Ownership and Management Changes

In 2007, Blakes Hotel was acquired by GuestInvest, a buy-to-let hotel investment firm, as part of its expansion strategy, though plans to adapt the property to its fractional ownership model did not materialize.[5][6] GuestInvest entered administration in October 2008 amid the global financial crisis, placing the hotel's future in jeopardy.[7][8] On September 3, 2010, the hotel was rescued from administration through Blakes Management Limited, formed by entrepreneurs Meir Abutbul and Navid Mirtorabi, who purchased it for approximately £20 million.[9][8] The new owners, both experienced in the hospitality sector—Abutbul through his pâtisserie chain Apostrophe and Mirtorabi via prior hotel investments—outlined ambitious plans to expand the Blakes brand internationally, including potential new properties in key global cities while preserving its boutique heritage.[10][11] Abutbul and Mirtorabi served as directors of Blakes Management Limited until March 31, 2021, when they resigned.[12] On the same date, Paul McCutcheon and Anh Nguyen Phan Thuy were appointed as directors, signaling a shift toward Vietnamese investment interests linked to TNK Hotels & Resorts.[12] Nguyen Phan Thuy briefly resigned in July 2021, replaced by Manh Cuong Nguyen, before her reappointment on August 1, 2024, following Cuong Nguyen's resignation; meanwhile, McCutcheon stepped down on February 27, 2023, with Adrian Gerard John Olivero appointed in his place.[12] These changes reflect ongoing management evolution under the Blakes Hotels group umbrella. The hotel faced temporary closure in December 2020 due to COVID-19 lockdowns in the UK, following a brief reopening in October 2020 with enhanced wellness protocols.[13][14] It resumed operations post-lockdown but closed again on May 31, 2022, for extensive renovations, originally slated to reopen by July 31, 2023; as of October 2024, it remains shuttered with no confirmed reopening date, leaving its operational future uncertain.[4]

## Location and Architecture

### Site in South Kensington

Blakes Hotel is located at 33 Roland Gardens, in the affluent South Kensington neighborhood of London's Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, an area renowned for its upscale residential character and cultural institutions.[1][15] The site occupies a quiet, tree-lined street that exemplifies the area's luxury vibe, where elegant Victorian architecture blends with modern exclusivity, attracting discerning residents and visitors alike.[2] Originally comprising a row of five Victorian townhouses constructed in the 19th century, the property reflects the neighborhood's historical development as a desirable enclave for London's elite during the Victorian era.[16] These townhouses were later converted into a hotel in 1978, preserving their period charm while adapting to contemporary hospitality needs.[15] South Kensington's prestige is further enhanced by its proximity to world-class landmarks, including the Victoria and Albert Museum, Natural History Museum, and Science Museum, all within a short walking distance, as well as the expansive green spaces of Hyde Park just to the north.[17][18] Accessibility to the site is convenient via public transport, with South Kensington Underground station approximately a 5- to 10-minute walk away, providing direct connections on the Piccadilly, District, and Circle lines to central London and beyond.[3][19] Note that as of 2024, the hotel has been temporarily closed since May 2022 for renovations, with reopening delayed and future uncertain.[1][4] This strategic positioning integrates Blakes seamlessly into South Kensington's vibrant yet serene atmosphere, balancing residential tranquility with easy access to the city's cultural and leisure offerings.[15]

### Building Structure and Exterior

Blakes Hotel occupies five interconnected Victorian townhouses at 33 Roland Gardens in South Kensington, London, forming a boutique property with 45 uniquely designed rooms and suites. These townhouses, originally constructed in the mid-19th century as part of the area's residential development, were acquired and transformed into the hotel in 1978 by designer and hotelier Anouska Hempel. The renovation process involved physically linking the separate structures through internal modifications to create a unified layout, while adhering to the architectural character of the original buildings.[20][3][21][22] The exterior presents a striking and dramatic presence, characterize

The frantic knocks rattle on the door of our hotel room at precisely 5.45am. The room is pitch black, but the red glow of the clock on a Bang and Olufsen television shines dimly at me through weary, half-open eyelids, assuring me that this is not my breakfast trolley. I struggle to get out of bed, but realise I’m tied up in an irregular trap of black satin and Egyptian cotton. Once free, I stumble out of bed nakedly and reach for a gown. A voice comes through the door, urgent and shrill, but with a thick, nicotine-stained sexiness that almost makes it okay…

“Open the door, open the door now!” “Okay, I’m coming, but sorry, who is it?” I mutter. “Don’t play games,” is the reply. “It’s Liz,” she whispers ominously. “Liz?” I murmur, lost and suddenly afraid; she’s still banging at my door. “Yes, come on, it’s English Liz. Don’t leave me here alone; I want to be in your bed.”

I falter, not sure what to say, or do, certain my now-stirring girlfriend would think opening the door somewhat imprudent. “Sorry, Liz, I think you have the wrong door,” I reply. “What? No… you’re naked, I can feel it, and you’re in there with someone else,” she purrs. And then: “Oh fuck…what…oh dear…sorry.” And with that, English Liz is gone, gone into what’s left of the warm September night.

Now, you might read my description of the above episode and think: what an irritation, what a bore. I’ll bet that if you’ve been to Blakes, you’ll read it with a wince and a wry smile. If you’ve ever been to Blakes, ever walked down the black and gold corridors, ever marvelled at the attention to detail and rare sense of grown-up, assured fun, you’ll probably be reaching for your leather-bound diary and earmarking a date for your next visit.

Blakes is a one-off, an oasis of louche charm within the safe confines of South Ken. The hotel stands well-proportioned and somewhat austere at the end of whitewashed row of stucco-fronted houses. A huge matte-black behemoth of a building, incongruous and unrepentant – like a stoned Keith Richards at a WI meeting. Blakes opened in 1978 and was arguably the first luxury boutique hotel in the world. The brain behind the hotel’s conception is Anoushka Hempel, a model and television-star-turned-hotelier who was born in 1941 on a ferry travelling from Papua New Guinea to New Zealand. The daughter of a travelling sheep farmer, she went on to become one of the world’s most influential hoteliers and the toast of London’s high society.

The hotel was built as a London bolthole for an international set of actors, musicians and minor royalty looking for stylish, discreet luxury away from the glare of the paparazzo’s camera. In 33 years, little of this ethos has changed; the hotel is so depended upon by certain clients that six-month residencies are not unheard of. There are 51 rooms and each is different. These are not subtle distinctions; each room is dramatically singular in its appeal. They range from elegant ‘Parisian’ singles right through to the 007 suite which comes fitted with a huge four-poster bed, a Victorian-style drawing room with fireplace, and a kitchenette – perfect for those looking for an extended stay.

The rooms are beautiful, set either side of long, vaguely mysterious corridors. There is a sense of a life well-travelled about the place: France, Italy, Russia and India all play their part in creating a sense of exoticism that allows you to leave Chelsea far behind. Our room is a well-appointed ‘director’s double’, a corner room with great views over the streets below. If there was a clear-cut theme, this room would be the ‘Italian stallion’, and perhaps that’s who usually stays here. Yes, that could be it: some wild, leather-faced, Viagra-laced lothario, drinking Campari and smoking Murratis, waiting for his English Liz to come a-knocking. The room is black and gold, with dark blinds, ancient Italianate etchings on the walls and a huge gilt-edged chariot bed, complete with hand-painted, golden rams locking horns at its base. If there was ever a room that screamed “I have endless reserves of testosterone pumping through bulging veins that remain discreetly hidden within the sleekest, smartest Savile Row suit”, then this is it.

We shower in the lap of luxury, then leave, feeling a little overwhelmed. Downstairs all is sultry yet calm; reds and blacks dominate as you climb down the thin staircase that leads from reception to the basement restaurant and bar. Here, in the bowels of the building, that traveller’s sense of adventure is in evidence once again. Ancient Louis Vuitton packing cases double up as tables and striking tribal art lines the walls of a bar which is invariably filled with elegantly dishevelled characters that look like they’ve seen and tasted a thing or two.

The restaurant has a feel of the Orient with Japanese-style wooden dividers and Chinese art and ceramics on display. Lighting is red and glowing – this is a place where it seems to be midnight all day. The menu is rather unique; it follows the East Asian form, but I have a feeling it has been assembled by someone with a weakness for the most decadent examples of French and Italian cuisine. Tortellini of Landes foie gras and truffled creamed potato sit next to Black Miso cod and Teriyaki beef. These initially discordant-seeming dishes actually work somehow to create a menu of depth and variety. It could be dreadful and clumsy, but rarely is; an understanding of quality ingredients helps it to work somehow.

The only dish that mis-hits is a side of creamed spinach, which has a rather unpleasant, murky aftertaste. The wine list is short but elegant and well-constructed, and offers some interesting wines from obscure producers. We enjoy a sprightly Sancerre followed by a modern-style ‘Super Tuscan’ from Bolgheri, which is excellent at a fraction of the price you’d pay for decent Brunello or the likes of Ornellaia. Like the hotel itself, the restaurant is elegant without being formal; it’s welcoming – the sort of place in which you could get hideously drunk without upsetting anyone.

It is only in the morning, as I lie reclining on black satin, eating scrambled eggs topped with shaved white truffles and ruminating on the dramas of the night that I realise that this is without doubt my favourite hotel in London. Nowhere else I’ve stayed in our capital feels quite this exciting. So what if the nights are broken by gravelly-voiced vixens looking for late night thrills? If it’s good enough for Micky Rourke and Gwyneth Paltrow, I’ll book in for a week and sleep when I’m dead.

# Blakes Hotel rescued from administration for £20m

By Rachel Johnson

04-Oct-2010 Last updated on 26-Apr-2021 at 21:11 GMT

## Luxury boutique hotel Blakes Hotel in London has been purchased out of administration for around £20 million, as its new owners announce plans to take the brand abroad

Luxury boutique hotel Blakes Hotel in London has been purchased out of administration for around £20million, as its new owners announce plans to take the brand abroad.

The iconic Mayfair hotel was bought by Navid Mirtorabi, hotelier and investor, Meir Abutbul founder of boulanger-patisserie chain Apostrophe.

The partners said they planned to expand the Blakes brand internationally, although nobody was available to comment on the details today.

The 51-room Blakes hotel is set to be redesigned by Anouska Hempel, the same designer who came up with the concept for the hotel when it first opened in 1981.

Meir Abutbul, director of the new management company Blakes Management, said: “Blakes is spectacular, it looks sumptuous, but we have some improvements in mind and Anouska Hempel will be in charge of design and styling. We are also planning on expanding the Blakes brand internationally.”

Abutbul’s first business in the UK was the café chain Apostrophe, and he owns two other hotels in London. Mirtorabi owns several other hotels in London.

Guestinvest

Blakes hotel hit trouble two years ago when parent company GuestInvest, the firm that pioneered "buy to let hotel rooms", fell into administration.

However Barclay's which financed the rescue deal for Blakes, said that the prospects were now good for the hotel.

Nick Worthington, relationship director at Barclays Corporate, said: “The London boutique sector continues to perform very strongly, and the opportunity that an expansion of Blakes presents is a compelling one.

He added: "It is great for us to be working alongside Navid and Meir as they take Blakes to the next level."

NEWS

The Blakes Hotel in London has been acquired for £20m, and its new owners are considering rolling out the brand across the globe.

The iconic, 52-bedroom hotel was bought out of administration by hoteliers and investors Navid Mirtorabi and Meir Abutbul.

The hotel - designed by Anouska Hempel in 1981 - was acquired by buy-to-let firm GuestInvest in 2007, but that company went into administration in 2008.

Meir Abutbul, who is the director of the new management company Blakes Management Limited, said: “Blakes is spectacular. It looks sumptuous, but we have some improvements in mind and Anouska Hempel will be in charge of design and styling. We are also planning on expanding the Blakes brand internationally.”

Blakes Hotel is a 5-star hotel in London, and considered one of the world's first boutique hotels. It is at 33 Roland Gardens in South Kensington. It was established by London hotelier and designer Anouska Hempel, and contains eclectic artifacts collected by Hempel during her international travels. Known for protecting its clients' privacy from the paparazzi, it often accommodates celebrities. Regular guests have included Princess Margaret; Sarah Ferguson; actresses Gwyneth Paltrow and Lindsay Lohan; and supermodel Kate Moss..

The hotel is also home to a Mediterranean-style restaurant and an exclusive lounge named Blakes Below, which debuted in 2016.

The hotel was mentioned as a plot point in Sofia Coppola's 2020 film On the Rocks.

The hotel has been closed since May 31, 2022.

# Blakes Hotel rescued from administration for £20m

By Rachel Johnson

04-Oct-2010 Last updated on 26-Apr-2021 at 21:11 GMT

## Luxury boutique hotel Blakes Hotel in London has been purchased out of administration for around £20 million, as its new owners announce plans to take the brand abroad

Luxury boutique hotel Blakes Hotel in London has been purchased out of administration for around £20million, as its new owners announce plans to take the brand abroad.

The iconic Mayfair hotel was bought by Navid Mirtorabi, hotelier and investor, Meir Abutbul founder of boulanger-patisserie chain Apostrophe.

The partners said they planned to expand the Blakes brand internationally, although nobody was available to comment on the details today.

The 51-room Blakes hotel is set to be redesigned by Anouska Hempel, the same designer who came up with the concept for the hotel when it first opened in 1981.

NEWS

The Blakes Hotel in London has been acquired for £20m, and its new owners are considering rolling out the brand across the globe.

The iconic, 52-bedroom hotel was bought out of administration by hoteliers and investors Navid Mirtorabi and Meir Abutbul.

The hotel - designed by Anouska Hempel in 1981 - was acquired by buy-to-let firm GuestInvest in 2007, but that company went into administration in 2008.

Meir Abutbul, who is the director of the new management company Blakes Management Limited, said: “Blakes is spectacular. It looks sumptuous, but we have some improvements in mind and Anouska Hempel will be in charge of design and styling. We are also planning on expanding the Blakes brand internationally.”
